FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 10,574 early adolescents aged 9-11 from 22 nationally distributed sites, ABCD 4.0 data release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional and longitudinal multivariable linear mixed models of 24-hour movement behavior adherence and cognitive, psychosocial, BMI, and brain morphometric outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No phone/SM-specific numeric estimate found anywhere in the paper. The exposure is adherence to a generic recreational screen time guideline (<=2 hours/day total), not decomposed by modality. Step 2: The screen time construct per the methods is an aggregate guideline-adherence dichotomy covering all recreational screen time (no per-modality breakdown into phone/SM vs TV vs gaming). No separate social media or phone time coefficient appears in any main table, figure, or supplement. Fails Step 2 because the composite bundles non-phone/non-SM activities without a separate breakout.

notes: Screen time here is a 24-hour movement guideline adherence variable (<=2 h/day total recreational screen time); no platform-specific breakdown is reported anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, multi-center prospective cohort, 11,875 children aged 9-10 from 21 U.S. recruitment sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional and longitudinal multivariable logistic regressions of cyberbullying victimization and substance initiation in ABCD data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: The sole digital-related variable is cyberbullying victimization (asked as experience of being bullied 'online, in texts, group texts, or on social media'). Per binding clarification, cyberbullying alone does not qualify unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable that has its own coefficient. No separate phone use, social media use, or screen time variable is analyzed with its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. NOT_MET.

notes: Cyberbullying victimization is the only digital-related exposure; no separate phone/SM variable with its own coefficient anywhere in the paper.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, 11,875 children at 21 sites; 5,027 adolescents included with Year 2 pre-pandemic and COVID-19 survey data.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal LMMs and GLMMs examining associations between social media use, video gaming, and sleep outcomes before and during COVID-19 pandemic.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article; no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: 'time spent on social media platforms' (a named SM variable) is reported as a significant predictor of sleep outcomes with separate chi-square statistics and simple slope coefficients throughout: TIB (chi-sq(1)=114.56, p<.01; simple slopes reported pre-pandemic and all 6 pandemic timepoints), bedtime (chi-sq(1)=456.52, p<.01; simple slopePre-pandemic=0.19, p<.01 at all assessments), sleep onset latency (chi-sq(1)=33.74, p<.01), and number of awakenings (chi-sq(1)=6.94, p=.008). Sleep is an individual-trait-level health outcome. FT-IC2 MET at Step 1.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple numeric results reported for social media use as IV predicting sleep outcomes (chi-square statistics, simple slopes with CIs for each assessment timepoint), e.g., simple slopePre-pandemic for bedtime = 0.19, p<.01.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analyses performed and numeric results reported for the SM-sleep relationship using LMM and GLMM frameworks.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media use is the IV (predictor) in all analyses, with sleep as the DV. FT-EC7 requires SM to be the DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere; that condition is not met here.
notes: Social media use (time on social media platforms) is treated as IV predicting sleep (TIB, bedtime, sleep onset latency, awakenings) throughout the paper. Single player video gaming is also included as a separate IV but is not a qualifying SM/phone variable on its own; the social media variable alone is sufficient for IC2 MET.
